evals.prompt: Synthesize themes from these 10 support tickets about a SaaS product: Tickets mention: 'slow performance during peak hours' (3 times), 'confusing reporting interface' (4 times), 'missing export to CSV' (2 times), 'pricing seems high for what we get' (1 time). What are the themes, and which should we prioritize?
evals.expected_output: Should identify 3 clear themes: (1) Performance issues - 30% of feedback (3 mentions), affects peak usage, impacts daily work; (2) Reporting confusion - 40% of feedback (4 mentions), affects user capability, frustration signal; (3) Feature gap (CSV export) - 20% of feedback (2 mentions); (4) Pricing concern - 10% (1 mention). Should prioritize: Reporting confusion (#1 - affects most users, quick-win to improve UX/docs), then Performance (ongoing issue requiring investigation), then CSV export (validate demand before building). Should note: Pricing complaint is outlier (only 1); not a trend yet, but worth monitoring.

evals.prompt: Compare feedback from churned customers vs. retained customers for an onboarding platform. Churned customers mention: 'setup took too long' (8 mentions), 'didn't see value immediately' (6 mentions), 'confusing UI' (5 mentions). Retained customers mention: 'easy to get started' (5 mentions), 'great support' (6 mentions), 'integrations we needed' (4 mentions). What's the difference?
evals.expected_output: Should identify key insight: churned customers struggle with entry experience (setup time, understanding value, UI clarity), while retained customers experience easy entry + good support + feature fit. Root cause: Onboarding experience determines whether customers reach 'first success' and stick around. Should recommend: (1) Analyze setup flow - can we streamline to reduce time? (2) Add guided value realization - show immediate benefit, not just features; (3) Improve UI clarity - review onboarding UX specifically; (4) Maintain support quality - retained customers credit support as differentiator. Should suggest A/B testing: streamlined onboarding + guided value path vs. current flow, measure time-to-first-success and retention.

evals.prompt: Translate this customer complaint into a product recommendation for the marketing team: 'I thought this product did X, but it turns out it doesn't. I paid for it and then found out it's not what I needed.'
evals.expected_output: Should translate for marketing: The product positioning is misleading or unclear; customers have incorrect expectations before purchase. Recommendation for marketing: (1) Update landing page to clarify what the product does and doesn't do; add specific examples of use cases and non-use cases; (2) Create comparison table showing [product] vs. similar tools and what sets us apart; (3) Improve FAQ with 'Is this right for me?' section; (4) Add customer testimonials from customers in [target segment] to show real-world use cases; (5) Create onboarding sequence that clarifies capabilities vs. expectations. This is a positioning/messaging problem, not a product problem. If many customers have this misunderstanding, it's a high-priority marketing fix because it affects churn.

evals.prompt: You have NPS follow-up responses from 50 customers. The detractors (score 0-6) mention: 'too expensive' (12), 'missing features I need' (8), 'support is slow' (5), 'switching to competitor' (7). The passives (7-8) mention: 'it works but nothing special' (10), 'good but could be better' (8). What insights do you prioritize?
evals.expected_output: Should prioritize by impact: (1) Detractors talking about switching to competitor - these are at-risk and actively considering alternatives. Recommendation: Understand which competitor and why (feature gap? better UX? better support?). This is retention-critical. (2) 'Too expensive' (12 detractors) - largest complaint. Investigate: Are these price-sensitive segments where we don't have product-market fit? Or are they not seeing value? Pricing review vs. value demonstration. (3) Missing features (8) - Validate which features are most requested; prioritize accordingly. (4) Support speed (5) - Operational fix; improve SLA/staffing. (5) Passives saying 'nothing special' (18 responses) - Expansion risk; need to drive deeper value realization and engagement. Should recommend: Separate at-risk detractors (switching) from price-sensitive ones; take different actions per segment.

evals.prompt: Create a monthly customer feedback summary for leadership covering: Overall sentiment, top 3 themes, quick wins, roadmap alignment. You have: 200 support tickets, 40 NPS responses, 15 customer interviews, and app store reviews (50 comments). What structure works best?
evals.expected_output: Should use structure: (1) Opening: Overall sentiment score (NPS, CSAT if available) and month-over-month trend; (2) Top 3 themes: List with frequency and severity; (3) Segment view: Do feedback themes vary by customer type? (4) Quick wins: 2-3 things we could fix this month (docs, UI, FAQ, etc.); (5) Roadmap alignment: Do feedback themes align with planned roadmap? Any surprises? (6) At-risk signals: Are any segments showing new complaints or declining satisfaction? (7) Source comparison: Does feedback differ across channels? (App store more negative? Interviews more detailed?) (8) Recommended actions: What should product/support/marketing prioritize? (9) Metrics: Track: Number of feedback items by source, sentiment distribution, themes trending up/down, customer segment patterns. Output in 1-page format with visuals (sentiment chart, theme bar chart, segment heatmap).

evals.prompt: We're implementing a feature that customers requested heavily (50+ mentions in feedback). How do we measure if it actually solved the problem and improved retention/satisfaction?
evals.expected_output: Should design measurement approach: (1) Pre-launch baseline: Measure current churn rate, NPS, CSAT for customers who mentioned this feature request; (2) Post-launch tracking: Track adoption of new feature (% using it, frequency, depth of usage); (3) Outcome metrics: Measure (a) Do customers who use the feature have lower churn? (b) Does NPS improve for users of the feature? (c) Do support tickets for this pain point decrease? (d) Do new feature requests related to this area decline? (4) Cohort analysis: Compare cohort that uses feature vs. cohort that doesn't - control for other variables; (5) Follow-up surveys: Ask customers who requested this: 'Does this solve your need? What's still missing?' (6) Timeline: Set expectation for measurement (minimum 30-60 days to see behavioral change, especially for retention). Should warn: Just because we built what customers asked for doesn't guarantee it solves the problem - need to validate it actually improves outcomes.

evals.prompt: Root cause analysis: Customers complain that 'onboarding is too complicated.' But when we dig deeper, are they saying: (a) Too many steps? (b) Steps are hard to understand? (c) Takes too long? (d) Not clear what the value is at the end? How do we diagnose which?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend targeted follow-up questions: (1) For 'too many steps': 'Which steps felt unnecessary or redundant?' and measure time spent per step; (2) For 'hard to understand': 'Which parts confused you?' and identify UX/copy issues; (3) For 'takes too long': 'How long did onboarding take you?' and measure against benchmark; (4) For 'not clear on value': 'After onboarding, did you understand what you could do with this?' Different root causes = different solutions: Too many steps = remove/consolidate; hard to understand = UX/copy improvements; takes too long = automation/skip option; unclear value = guided success path showing first win. Should recommend: Conduct 5-10 customer interviews with 'onboarding is complicated' feedback, ask these diagnostic questions, map to specific UX fixes. Don't assume all complaints have same root cause.

evals.prompt: You notice a new complaint appearing in feedback from enterprise customers: 'We need advanced permission/role management.' It's only appeared 3 times in the last month. Is this a trend we should act on, or just noise?
evals.expected_output: Should evaluate signal strength: (1) Frequency: 3 mentions is small, but consider source - are these from high-value accounts? (2) Segment: All 3 from enterprise? That's significant for enterprise strategy even if total count is low. (3) Timing: Is this new (trend) or recurring pattern? (4) Importance: Enterprise customers typically have outsized value, so even 1-3 requests from them can be high-priority. (5) Validation: Ask: 'Are other enterprise customers also asking but not mentioning?' - talk to sales team. Should recommend: Don't ignore based on low count. Instead, (a) Talk to the 3 customers directly - understand severity and timeline urgency; (b) Ask sales if they're hearing this in deals; (c) If 2+ customers mention this is blocking deals, prioritize it; if it's nice-to-have, track it as emerging trend. Enterprise feedback often has high signal-to-noise ratio because accounts are fewer but higher value.
